pi_items.source: Verified in setup_task.sh (line 11) and export_result.sh (line 8) - consistent throughout codebase

Downloaded and extracted from GitHub release https://github.com/invesalius/invesalius3/releases/download/v3.0/0051.zip. DICOM header confirms patient name 'CT 0051 - InVesalius Sample' and files located in '0051' subdirectory

Verifier.py line 98 uses this value as default. Verifier comments (line 9) indicate filled skull target is ~1300-2200 cm3. Measurement of actual bone is ~871 cm3, plus estimated internal cavity would be needed to verify this target
Verifier.py line 99 uses this value as default. Verifier comments (line 9) indicate filled skull target is ~1300-2200 cm3. This appears consistent with claimed upper bound

Direct measurement of DICOM dataset using HU 226-3071 threshold (per task description). Measured bone volume: 870.7 cm³ from all 108 slices (pixel spacing 0.4785 mm, slice thickness 2.0 mm, voxel volume 0.458 mm³)

Direct measurement of soft tissue in DICOM dataset (HU 0-500). Soft tissue volume measured as 2957.6 cm³. This exceeds the minimum claim of 2400.0 cm³, suggesting the metadata value may be conservative

privileged_info_summary: The CT Cranium DICOM dataset (series 0051) is real and publicly available from InVesalius GitHub releases. The DICOM data contains 108 slices (512x512 pixels) with pixel spacing 0.4785 mm and slice thickness 2.0 mm. Using the Bone preset threshold range of 226-3071 HU (as specified in the task description), the measured bone volume is 870.7 cm³, which exceeds the metadata claim of hollow_bone_max_vol_cm3 of 600.0 cm³ by 45%. The soft tissue volume (HU 0-500) measures 2957.6 cm³, which is above the claimed minimum of 2400.0 cm³. The task output path (/home/ga/Documents/solid_cranium.stl) and DICOM series identifier (ct_cranium/0051) are correctly specified. The volume range expectations (1200-2200 cm³ after Fill Holes operation) appear technically reasonable based on typical cranial anatomy, though they cannot be fully verified without executing the actual InVesalius image processing workflow.
